content: My main personal project outside of this is neli. I have a fair amount of experience looking at netlink kernel code and I'm wondering if one of the ways I could be helpful is working on netlink support in Rust for Linux. I understand that mainly right now we're looking into driver support so netlink does not necessarily fall under that. What is everyone's thoughts here?

In theory, you can write a crate/library that kernel and user space can share, right? Because based on my limited understanding of netlink (Googling it in 20 minutes), it requires a protocal implementation between user and kernel. If so, that's something.

Sharing kernel/userspace Rust code is something that Kent Overstreet was interested in too.

Drivers are important, but networking bits (and other things) are also useful. @Wedson Almeida Filho worked a bit on networking filters support, for instance.
And by "a bit" I mean a working netfilter sample and an echo server with async!

@John Baublitz one thing that I started looking into (but paused for a bit) was replacing ksmbd handling of networking code with rust async. They use netlink to talk to a userspace daemon, so if you'd like to add abstractions for that, I think it would be great and I'd be happy to work with you.

If it plugged into async mode, it would be even better.

that's actually one of the next todos in my library, adding better async support. there's some currently, but it's incomplete. I had also discussed making neli able to run in kernel and userspace with @Josh Triplett and I'm more than willing to have someone work with me on getting it kernel ready if that's of interest. totally happy to add maintainers.
the one thing that I don't know about is whether the license is okay. it's BSD 3 Clause.
another note is that I added proc macros that depend on syn and quote for deriving serialization and deserialization, so I'm unsure whether that's a non starter based on the previous meeting. :)

John Baublitz said:

the one thing that I don't know about is whether the license is okay. it's BSD 3 Clause.

BSD-3-Clause is a "preferred" license in the kernel, it should be fine.

John Baublitz said:

another note is that I added proc macros that depend on syn and quote for deriving serialization and deserialization, so I'm unsure whether that's a non starter based on the previous meeting. :)

At least before the merge, likely :) But after the merge, we will decide what to do there. We may just merge them. Perhaps to be discussed in Kangrejos/LPC.

Miguel Ojeda said:

John Baublitz said:

another note is that I added proc macros that depend on syn and quote for deriving serialization and deserialization, so I'm unsure whether that's a non starter based on the previous meeting. :)

At least before the merge, likely :) But after the merge, we will decide what to do there. We may just merge them. Perhaps to be discussed in Kangrejos/LPC.

I think there's a lot of work to do regardless on my library, but I'd be more than happy to work on that and get it cleaned up if it can be helpful in the kernel. that was always one of my goals for this project anyway.

I don't know much about neli, but given that it's for user space, I assume it's more focused on the client slide of netlink. In the kernel we care more about the server side; do you have a sense of how much overlap there is between the two cases?

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

I don't know much about neli, but given that it's for user space, I assume it's more focused on the client slide of netlink. In the kernel we care more about the server side; do you have a sense of how much overlap there is between the two cases?

in terms of shared code, there is some. for example, a lot of the code is defining interfaces for handling data structure serialization and deserialization. the socket code will be virtually useless in the kernel version but the data structures may be useful. I specifically opted against bindgen for my library which has some benefits and drawbacks. the major benefits are around things like:

  • constants: they're all defined as enums so it makes the usability a little bit better in terms of knowing what constants to use for what arguments, etc.
  • data structure deserialization: I've caught bugs in the kernel before with my library because the parsing ensures that packets are valid according to the outlined protocol. because I'm using higher level data structures, it basically does verification on much of the packets during deserialization to ensure they're valid.

the major drawback is pretty obvious, having to update data structures in two places and maintain two separate codebases.
my initial thought is that I could split it into three crates: neli-shared, neli-kernel and neli-userspace
also, this is definitely more of a long-term goal so I'm happy to do some work just in the kernel library for now.

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

John Baublitz one thing that I started looking into (but paused for a bit) was replacing ksmbd handling of networking code with rust async. They use netlink to talk to a userspace daemon, so if you'd like to add abstractions for that, I think it would be great and I'd be happy to work with you.

If it plugged into async mode, it would be even better.

What would you recommend for getting started on this?

Neli is a great library, enjoyed using it :)

BTW: @John Baublitz did you see my dummy_rs network driver? It was written before async, and has to have a major rework, before being in a state to be merged, but maybe a bit interesting #439

I'd recommend looking at rust/kernel/net.rs and maybe extending it to support netlink if it makes sense. Also rust/kernel/kasync/net.rs for the async version. AFAIU, netlink doesn't offer a socket interface similar to the other sockets in the kernel, it's more callback-based, which may not be very good for async, OTOH, if kernel servers normally just hand the new data over to another thread/work-item, it may still make sense to hook it up with async and have the callback act as a reactor that wakes up the async task.
